coredata.dc:title: Positron emission tomography (PET) guided glioblastoma targeting by a fullerene-based nanoplatform with fast renal clearance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Yayun
Yang, Dongzhi
Lu, Weifei
Hu, Xiongwei
Hong, Hao
Cai, Ting
coredata.dc:description:
Various carbonaceous nanomaterials, including fullerene, carbon nanotube, graphene, and carbon dots, have attracted increasing attention during past decades for their potential applications in biological imaging and therapy. In this study, we have developed a fullerene-based tumor-targeted positron emission tomography (PET) imaging probe. Water-soluble functionalized C60 conjugates were radio-labeled with 64Cu and modified with cyclo (Arg-Gly-Asp) peptides (cRGD) for targeting of integrin α vβ3 in glioblastoma. The specificity of fluorescein-labeled C60 conjugates against cellular integrin α vβ3 was evaluated in U87MG (integrin α vβ3 positive) and MCF-7 cells (integrin α vβ3 negative) by confocal fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry. Our results indicated that cRGD-conjugated C60 derivatives showed better cellular internalization compared with C60 derivatives without the cRGD attachment. Moreover, an interesting finding on intra-nuclei transportation of cRGD-conjugated C60 derivatives was observed in U87MG cells. In vivo serial PET studies showed preferential accumulation of cRGD-conjugated C60 derivatives at in U87MG tumors. In addition, the pharmacokinetic profiles of these fullerene-based nanoparticles conjugated with cRGD and 1,4,7-triazacyclononane-1,4,7-triacetic acid (NOTA) fit well with the three compartment model. The renal clearance of C60-based nanoparticles is remarkably fast, which makes this material very promising for safer cancer theranostic applications.
Statement of significance Safety is one of the major concerns for nanomedicine and nanomaterials with fast clearance profile are highly desirable. Fullerene is a distinct type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with ultrasmall size, uniform dispersity, and versatile reactivity. Here we have developed a fullerene-based tumor-targeted positron emission tomography imaging probe using water-soluble functionalized C60 conjugates radio-labeled with 64Cu and modified with cyclo (Arg-Gly-Asp) peptides (cRGD) for glioblastoma targeting. The improved tumor targeting property along with fast renal clearance behavior of C60-based nanoparticles makes this material very promising for future safer cancer theranostic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fullerene
Positron emission tomography
Cancer targeting
Integrin α vβ3

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst pubtype ref specialabst
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2017 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design, preparation, and characterization of C60 conjugates
In vitro investigation of C60 conjugates

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical Characterization of C60 derivatives by (A) FT-IR spectra of C60, C60[C(COOEt)2]3, C60[C(COOH)2]3, and C60-PEG; (B) Thermo gravimetric analysis curves of C60[C(COOH)2]3, 8arm-PEG-NH2, and C60-PEG; (C) UV–vis spectra of C60[C(COOH)2]3, 8arm-PEG-NH2, and C60-PEG. Inset: UV–vis spectra of C60-PEG, Fluorescein-C60-PEG, and free FITC in aqueous solution; and (D) 1H NMR spectra of NOTA-C60-PEG and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD in D2O.

Morphological characterizations of C60 nanoconjugates: (A) Atomic force microscopy images showing an average size distribution of 100–120nm; (B) Dynamic light scattering analysis of NOTA-C60-PEG (black line, 106nm) and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD (red line, 142nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cellular evaluation of fluorescein-C60-PEG conjugates for targeting of integrin αvβ3. Fluorescein-C60-PEG and fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD conjugates are incubated with U87MG (integrin αvβ3 +) and MCF-7 (integrin αvβ3 −) cells at the concentration of 25 and 100µg/mL for 0.5h. Both (A) flow cytometry and (B) confocal fluorescence microscopy imaging are used to study the interaction of these fluorescein-labeled C60 conjugates with these two cell lines. The green fluorescence is from fluorescein, and the blue fluorescence is from DAPI (nuclei staining). The concentration was 15μg/ml. Scale bars: 50μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

64Cu labeling of C60 conjugates and their in vivo investigation by PET. (A) Serum stability evaluation of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD and 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG showing more than 91% of 64Cu retained on the C60 conjugates after 24h-incubation in complete mouse serum. (B) Serial PET images of U87MG tumor-bearing mice at different time-points after injected with 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD and 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG. The yellow triangles indicate the location of the tumor. The region-of-interest (ROI) quantification analysis of radioactivity accumulation in major organs was also conducted for (C) 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD and (D) 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG. (E) Comparison of tumor uptake at various time points post-injection for 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD and 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG was given. The data were shown as mean±SD. (n=3; *p <0.05). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Time-dependent blood concentrations of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD in mice after i.v. administration (n=3). The measured data were better described by the three compartment model.

Ex vivo evaluation of C60 conjugates. (A) Organ biodistribution of 64Cu-labeled C60 conjugates by gamma-counting measurement at 24h p.i. (n=3 for all group). (B) Ex vivo histology analysis. Immunohistological staining of tumor and major tissues from mice injected with fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD and fluorescein-C60-PEG. The expression pattern of integrin αvβ3 (red fluorescence) was correlated with fluorescein (green fluorescence) indicating the existence of C60 conjugates. Scale bar: 100μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Simulation results of different compartment models and weights.
F-test of different compartment models and weights.
Pharmacokinetic parameters of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD in mice post intravenous administration by three-compartmental open model with weight of 1/cc.

head.ce:title: Positron emission tomography (PET) guided glioblastoma targeting by a fullerene-based nanoplatform with fast renal clearance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines, School of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Drug Discovery for Metabolic Diseases, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Drug Discovery for Metabolic Diseases

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Molecular Imaging, Department of Radiology, University of Michigan, Michigan 48109-2200, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Center for Molecular Imaging, Department of Radiology, University of Michigan, MI 48109-2200, USA (H. Hong) and State Key Laboratory of Natural Medicines, School of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China (T. Cai).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Various carbonaceous nanomaterials, including fullerene, carbon nanotube, graphene, and carbon dots, have attracted increasing attention during past decades for their potential applications in biological imaging and therapy. In this study, we have developed a fullerene-based tumor-targeted positron emission tomography (PET) imaging probe. Water-soluble functionalized C60 conjugates were radio-labeled with 64Cu and modified with cyclo (Arg-Gly-Asp) peptides (cRGD) for targeting of integrin α vβ3 in glioblastoma. The specificity of fluorescein-labeled C60 conjugates against cellular integrin α vβ3 was evaluated in U87MG (integrin α vβ3 positive) and MCF-7 cells (integrin α vβ3 negative) by confocal fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry. Our results indicated that cRGD-conjugated C60 derivatives showed better cellular internalization compared with C60 derivatives without the cRGD attachment. Moreover, an interesting finding on intra-nuclei transportation of cRGD-conjugated C60 derivatives was observed in U87MG cells. In vivo serial PET studies showed preferential accumulation of cRGD-conjugated C60 derivatives at in U87MG tumors. In addition, the pharmacokinetic profiles of these fullerene-based nanoparticles conjugated with cRGD and 1,4,7-triazacyclononane-1,4,7-triacetic acid (NOTA) fit well with the three compartment model. The renal clearance of C60-based nanoparticles is remarkably fast, which makes this material very promising for safer cancer theranostic applications.

Safety is one of the major concerns for nanomedicine and nanomaterials with fast clearance profile are highly desirable. Fullerene is a distinct type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with ultrasmall size, uniform dispersity, and versatile reactivity. Here we have developed a fullerene-based tumor-targeted positron emission tomography imaging probe using water-soluble functionalized C60 conjugates radio-labeled with 64Cu and modified with cyclo (Arg-Gly-Asp) peptides (cRGD) for glioblastoma targeting. The improved tumor targeting property along with fast renal clearance behavior of C60-based nanoparticles makes this material very promising for future safer cancer theranostic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of the first fullerene C60 in 1985 [1], carbonaceous nanomaterials have attracted much attention during the past decades for various potential applications due to their structural uniqueness [2–5]. Recently, great progress has been made in utilizing carbonaceous nanomaterials (e.g., carbon nanotubes, graphene derivatives and carbon dots etc.) for biomedical imaging and therapeutic cargo delivery [6–8]. Among these many carbonaceous nanomaterials, fullerene is a distinct type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with ultrasmall size, uniform dispersity, and versatile reactivity [9]. Due to its attractive physicochemical properties, numerous researchers have contributed to the development of fullerene-based nanoconjugates for various biological applications, for example asantioxidant agents, photodynamic therapy mediators, drug or gene delivery carriers and theranostic agents [10–13].

Safety concerns are rising during the development of inorganic nanomaterials for biological applications. Particularly, according to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), all of the injectable nanomaterials should be cleared in a reasonable time frame to avoid the potential toxicity from long-term retention [14,15]. In the literature, it has been demonstrated that the size, shape and surface properties of nanoparticles will significantly affect their in vivo pharmacokinetic behavior including organ distribution, clearance pathway, and metabolic speed [16–20]. For instance, functionalized silica-based nanoparticles smaller than 5.5nm can effectively escape the capture of the mononuclear phagocytic system (MPS) and be cleared through the renal system after administration [15]. In contrast to carbon nanotubes and graphenes, C60 molecule is well defined as a highly symmetrical sphere with the van der Waals diameter of 0.95–1.04nm, which is expected to exhibit different pharmacokinetic profile in biological systems [21]. Integrin αvβ3 is chosen as the cancer-targeting receptor here due to its abundant expression in both tumor cells and vasculatures from different cancer types [22]. Integrin αvβ3 plays a critical role in both tumor growth and metastasis. It is over-expressed on most solid tumor types including glioblastoma, melanoma, breast, prostate, and ovarian cancer [23,24]. Integrin αvβ3 is also often up-regulated in tumors post-radiotherapy. The high-affinity interaction between arginine-glycine-asparate (RGD) motif-containing peptides (e.g., cyclo(RGDyK), cyclo(RGDfK), cyclo(RGDfC), etc.) and integrin αvβ3 has led to enormous interests in utilizing cRGD peptides for cancer theranostic applications [25]. In several previous reports, the strategy of either radiolabeling or fluorophore incorporation onto cRGD peptides has been successfully adopted for non-invasive tumor-targeted imaging [26–28].

In this study, our goal is to develop tumor-targeted C60 conjugates via a bottom-up synthetic chemistry, which can be detectable by positron emission tomography (PET) imaging. Highly water dispersable carboxylfullerenes were covalently conjugated with eight-arm amine-terminal branched polyethylene glycol (PEG), which can be further attached by various functional entities (PET isotope chelator, active tumor targeting ligand, etc.). cRGD was covalently attached onto C60 aggregates for selective identification of glioblastoma. To evaluate the targeting efficiency and pharmacokinetics of these C60 conjugates, the distribution and clearance behaviors of C60 conjugates in tumor-bearing mice were investigated by PET imaging at various time points, where 64Cu (t1/2: 12.7h) was incorporated into the structure of the C60 conjugates [26,27,29].

Fullerene (C60, purity >99.9%) and 1,8-Diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Diethyl bromomalonate and Sodium hydride (NaH) was acquired from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloide (EDCI) and Hydroxy-2,5-dioxopyrrolidine-3-sulfonicacid sodium salt (Sulfo-NHS) were purchased from Bide Pharmatech Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Eight-arm branched polyethylene glycol-amine (8-arm-PEG-NH2, 10kDa) was obtained from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (Changchun, China). Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was purchased from Melone Pharmaceutical Corporation (Dalian, China). (S)-2-(4-isothiocyanatobenzyl)-1,4,7-triazacyclononane-1,4,7-triacetic acid (p-SCN-Bn-NOTA, abbreviated as NOTA) was purchased from Macrocyclics, Inc. (Dallas, TX, USA). 4-Maleimidobutyricacid N-hydroxysuccimide ester (GMBS) and Tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP·HCl) were provided by Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). Cyclo (Arg-Gly-Asp-D-Phe-Cys) peptide (cRGDfC, abbreviated as cRGD) was purchased from Top-peptide Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 64CuCl2 was acquired from the cyclotron group at University of Wisconsin. Anti-integrin αvβ3 antibody (catalog number: ab78289) was purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, MA, USA). Chelex 100 resin (50–100 mesh) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) and PD-10 size exclusion columns were acquired from GE Healthcare (Piscataway, NJ, USA). Alexa Fluor 488-labeled rabbit anti-mouse IgGs were purchased from Lampire biological laboratories (Pipersville, PA, USA). Other reagents were purchased from Fisher Thermo Scientific. All chemicals, unless otherwise stated, were purchased from commercial sources and directly used as received without further purification. All buffers were prepared from Millipore-grade water with the pretreatment of Chelex 100 resin to minimize heavy metal contents in the aqueous solution.

FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo) and 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker 300MHz instrument. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano ZS-90, Malvern Instrument, USA) was used for characterizing hydrodynamic (HD) size and size distribution, as well as ζ-potentials of C60 conjugates at the concentration of 0.2mg/ml (based on C60). Atomic force microscope (AFM) measurement was carried out on Bruker Dimension ICON atomic force microscope (Bruker, Billerica, MA).

The trimalonic acid modified C60 derivative was synthesized following the procedures reported previously with minor modifications. C60 was initially reacted with diethyl bromomalonate via Bingle cyclopropanation in the presence of DBU, then hydrolyzed by NaH to yield water-soluble fullerene derivatives [30,31].

Briefly, in a 100ml dry three-necked flask equipped with a gas inlet, 50mL dropping funnel, and magnetic stirrer, 253mg C60 (0.35mmol) was dissolved in 50mL of dry toluene. Subsequently, 200μL diethyl bromomalonate (1mmol) were added followed by the dropwise addition of 173μL DBU (1mmol) diluted in 10mL of dry toluene. The reaction was carried out at room temperature with continuous stirring for 10h under argon. The trimalonic ester modified C60 was separated by silica gel chromatography and a rotary evaporator was used to remove the solvent.

The purified trimalonic ester modified C60 (120mg, 0.1mmol) was re-dissolved in 50mL of dry toluene followed by adding 90mg NaH (2.0mmol, 20 equiv.). The mixture was stirred and refluxed at 80°C for 10h. After the removal of the heating source, 5mL of MeOH was added immediately to quench the reaction. Red precipitate was collected from centrifugation and repeatedly washed by toluene and hexane. The red solid was added into 4M HCl and the resulting precipitate was repeatedly washed by 4M HCl and deionized water. The precipitate was further dried under vacuum to yield the expected trimalonic acid modified C60 derivative.

5mg trimalonic acid modified C60 derivative (C60[C(COOH)2]3, 0.005mmol) was dissolved in 2mL of anhydrous DMSO. EDCI/Sulfo-NHS (0.015mmol) as the coupling reagents was added into the DMSO solution. The solution was stirred for 15min at room temperature to yield C60 carboxylate active ester intermediate. Afterward, the above solution was added dropwise into 20mL of 0.1M phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) containing 8-arm-PEG-NH2 (150mg, 0.015mmol) and stirred for another 6h. The unreacted materials and reagents were removed by dialysis against deionized (DI) water for 48h using a cellulose dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut off (MWCO)=10kDa) to obtain the desired product, C60-PEG.

10mg of C60-PEG in 2ml of 0.1M carbonate buffer solution (pH 9.0) was incubated with 200μL DMSO containing 0.6mg p-SCN-Bn-NOTA (1μmol) to obtain NOTA-C60-PEG. An ultrafiltration centrifugal device (MWCO, 10kDa) was used to purify the resulting NOTA-C60-PEG. To this, GMBS (6mg, 20μmol) was added and reacted for 2h at pH 7.4, resulting in NOTA-C60-PEG-Mal. The excess of GMBS were removed by centrifugation filtration, NOTA-C60-PEG-Mal was then immediately reacted with an excess amount of cRGD at pH 7.4 overnight in the presence of TCEP·HCl to avoid disulfide formation between cRGD molecules. The final product NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD was purified by centrifugation filtration.

To investigate the effects of cRGD targeting ligands imposed on fullerene during cellular interactions, fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD was also synthesized following the same procedures as in the synthesis of NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD, since FITC and p-SCN-Bn-NOTA both have the SCN group which can mediate the chemical reaction with the NH2 group on C60-PEG. As controls, the non-targeted groups, NOTA-C60-PEG and fluorescein-C60-PEG were prepared as well.

The pH of 64CuCl2 (∼74MBq, in 0.1M HCl) was initially adjusted with 0.1M sodium acetate (pH 6.5). With the addition of 50μg of NOTA-C60-PEG or NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD, the final pH for the reaction mixture was kept within the range of 4 – 5.5. The reaction usually took 0.5–1h at 37°C with constant shaking (350rpm). 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD or 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG was purified from PD-10 with PBS being the mobile phase.

For serum stability studies, 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD or 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG was incubated in fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37°C for up to 24h (same time period as PET imaging). Fixed volume of the mixture (∼20μL per sample) were taken at different time points (n=3 per time points) and filtered through 100kDa cutoff Amicon filters. The radioactivity in collected filtrates was measured in a WIZARD2 gamma counter (Perkins-Elmer). The percentages of retained (i.e., intact) 64Cu on the C60 conjugates (64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD or 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG) were calculated using the equation [(total radioactivity−filtrate radioactivity)/total radioactivity×100%].

U87MG cells (Integrin αvβ3-overexpressed) and MCF-7 cells (integrin αvβ3-low) were suspended in cold PBS supplemented with 2% bovine serum albumin with a final concentration of 2×106 cells/mL [32]. After 0.5h- and 2h-incubation with fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD and fluorescein-C60-PEG (25μg/mL based on C60) at 37°C, the cells were washed repeatedly with cold PBS to remove the unbound C60 conjugates. Afterwards, with the resuspension in PBS, the cells were analyzed using a BD LSR Fortessa four-color analysis cytometer (Becton-Dickinson, San Jose, CA), equipped with 488 and 633nm laser. FlowJo (version, X.0.7, Tree Star Inc.) was used to analyze the cellular fluorescence. Cells were also examined under a Nikon A1 confocal fluorescence microscope to validate the flow cytometric results.

U87MG cells (from the American type culture collection, Manassas, VA) were maintained in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM, Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum at 37°C with appropriate humidity and 5% carbon dioxide. Cells were used at ∼75% confluence. All animal procedures were performed according to a protocol approved by University of Michigan Committee on Use and Care of Animals (UCUCA, protocol number: PRO00006023). Murine tumor model was established by subcutaneous (s.c.) injection of 5×106 U87MG cells in 100μL mixture of 50% PBS and 50% Matrigel into the right flank of 6-week-old female nude mice (Charles River). Mice were used for imaging studies when the tumor diameter was 5–8mm (typically 4weeks after inoculation).

The mice bearing U87MG tumors (integrin αvβ3 +) were intravenously injected with 7±3 megabecquerel (MBq, n=8) of 64Cu-labeled C60 conjugates, and static PET scans of 40 million events per scan were performed on an Inveon microPET/CT (Siemens Medical Solutions USA, Inc.) at the selected time-points (0.5, 2, 16, and 24h) post-injection (p.i.). All the images were reconstructed through a 3D ordered subset expectation maximization (3D OSEM) algorithm, with no applied attenuation or scatter correction. The three-dimensional region-of-interest (ROIs) were imposed on the tumor and major organs (e.g. liver, heart, muscle) from decay-corrected whole-body images via the Inveon Research Workplace (IRW, v 4.2) [33]. The radioactivity density from ROIs were finally converted to MBq per gram (MBq/g) by the assumption of 1g/ml in tissue density along with a conversion factor. Subsequently, the organ radioactivity density was then divided by the total administered radioactivity to obtain percentage injected dose per gram of tissue (%ID/g). After the last PET scan at 24h p.i., organ distribution studies were executed to corroborate the material uptake profile with PET. The blood, tumors, and major organs/tissues from the mice were collected and wet-weighed post euthanasia. The radioactivity density in each organ/tissue was measured by the WIZARD2 gamma counter and calculated finally into%ID/g (means±SD).

In order to determine the circulation half-life of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD, three separate mice were subjected to a 30-min dynamic PET acquisition (time frame: 1min×5+2.5min×10) [33]. The one-compartmental (Eq. (1)), two compartmental (Eq. (2)) and three compartmental models (Eq. (3)) were adopted to analyze the data. In Eq. (1), c 0 is the initial blood concentration, c stands for the blood concentration, k represents the elimination rate constant, and t indicates the time. In Eq. (2), α is the distributional rate constant, β is the eliminative rate constant, c is the blood concentration, t is the time, A and B are empirical constants. In Eq. (3), α and β is the distributional rate constant, γ is the eliminative rate constant, c is the blood concentration, t is the time, A, B and C are empirical constants.
(1)
c = c 0 ·
e
- kt
(2)
c = A ·
e
- α t
+ B ·
e
- β t
(3)
c = A ·
e
- α t
+ B ·
e
- β t
+ C ·
e
- γ t

The Drug and statistics (DAS) software (Version 2.0, Nanjing University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, China) was used to fit the drug concentration-vs-time data to the compartmental models described above.

Separate groups of mice (n=2 per group) were injected with fluorescein-labeled C60 conjugates (30μg, ∼1.5mg/kg) and euthanized at 3h post-injection (maximal tumor accumulation from PET). Tissues were collected and cut into frozen slices of 6μm in thickness. After cold acetone fixation for 10min, the tissues were rinsed with PBS and blocked by 2% BSA for 30min. The tissue slides were subsequently stained for integrin αvβ3 with the primary antibody (2μg/mL) for 1h, followed by Cy3-labeled rabbit anti-mouse IgG (2.5μg/mL) for 2h. The distribution of C60 conjugates were visualized by green fluorescence from fluorescein. All fluorescence images were taken with a Nikon A1 confocal microscope with a magnitude of 200×.

Data were presented as mean±standard deviation. Statistical significance was performed by Student’s t test and F test. Statistical significance was set at the P values less than 0.05, which were identified by asterisks in figures and tables.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, preparation, and characterization of C60 conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: Four primary conjugates of C60 were prepared and investigated: NOTA-C60-PEG, NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD, fluorescein-C60-PEG, and fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD. A schematic reaction protocol of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD is shown in Scheme 1 . The two conjugates with fluorescein tags (i.e., fluorescein-C60-PEG, and fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD) were primarily employed for in vitro evaluation of integrin αvβ3 targeting specificity using fluorescence microscopy. The two conjugates attached with NOTA (i.e., NOTA-C60-PEG and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD) were subsequently reacted with 64Cu for PET imaging and organ distribution studies.

It is well known that the pristine C60 is highly hydrophobic. In order to make it water-dispersible, the carboxylfullerene C60[C(COOH)2]3 was synthesized through the well-established Bingle-Hirsch cycloaddition and followed by ester hydrolysis (Scheme 1). FT-IR spectroscopy is often used to characterize the functionality of C60 derivatives [34–35]. Compared with the pristine C60, the FT-IR spectrum of C60[C(COOEt)2]3 shows peaks at 2977–2867cm−1 and ∼1743cm−1, which are attributed for CH3 & CH2 and CO stretch, respectively (Fig. 1 A and Fig. S1). In addition, 1H NMR (Fig. S2), ESI-MS (Fig. S3), and TGA analysis (Fig. S4) all confirmed that the intermediate C60[C(COOEt)2]3 was successfully acquired post DBU reaction. Meanwhile, in the spectrum of C60[C(COOH)2]3, the absence of the peaks at 2977–2867cm−1 for CH3 & CH2 stretch, and the peak shift of ester carbonyl CO stretch (∼1743cm−1) to acid carbonyl CO stretch (∼1720cm−1), indicate the formation of carboxylic groups via ester hydrolysis (Fig. 1A).

In this study, eight-arm branched PEG with amine on the end was further conjugated to C60[C(COOH)2]3 via amide formation (Scheme 1). Covalent conjugation of nanoparticles with branched PEG not only renders high aqueous solubility but also supplies ample functional groups (i.e., NH2) for further reactions with various functional entities, such as imaging and targeting ligand, etc [36–38]. The successful PEGylation of C60[C(COOH)2]3 is evidenced by FT-IR spectroscopy analysis. Compared with C60[C(COOH)2]3, C60-PEG shows a red shift from carboxyl CO stretch (∼1720cm−1) to amide CO stretch (∼1655cm−1), along with the observation of a new peak at ∼1105cm−1 attributed for C-O-C stretch (Fig. 1A).

The relative amount of PEG grafted onto C60 is determined by thermal gravimetric Analysis (TGA). As shown in Fig. 1B, the TGA curve of C60[C(COOH)2]3 exhibits a slow decomposition upon heating, reaching a plateau at ∼450°C with a 30% weight loss. However, for the pure PEG and C60-PEG, both of the curves exhibit sharp weight losses at 350∼400°C, showing about 1.8% and 5.7% residue left when reach plateaus, respectively. Thus, the molar ratio of PEG to C60 is calculated to be approximately 1∼2. The UV–vis spectrum of C60-PEG shows similar absorption pattern with those of C60[C(COOH)2]3, confirming the existence of C60 motif in the nanoparticles after the PEGylation (Fig. 1C).

The hetero-bifunctional crosslinking reagent GMBS is grafted to the primary amine group via the highly reactive succinimidyl ester terminus to generate NOTA-C60-PEG-Mal. The thiol-containing cRGD (i.e., cRGDfC) is subsequently conjugated to obtain NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD based on the thiol-maleimide reaction chemistry. For in vivo PET imaging, radiolabeling with 64Cu is performed to yield the 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD nanoconjugates. The 1H NMR spectra of NOTA-C60-PEG and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD are shown in Fig. 1D. The proton NMR spectrum of NOTA-C60-PEG (the upper graph) exhibits multiple peaks at 7.10–7.30ppm ascribed to protons from the aromatic ring of NOTA, suggesting the successful conjugation of NOTA to the C60-PEG. For NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD (the bottom one), the protons peaks attributed to the benzyl group and methylene groups of the cRGD peptide are observed at 7.10–7.30ppm and 1.50–3.50ppm, respectively. Meanwhile, fluorescein-labeled samples with the same size and surface chemistry were prepared for the in vitro cellular investigation and ex vivo organ distribution study.

The hydrodynamic diameters and surface charges of synthesized C60 conjugates are measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and ζ-potential after each step of conjugation. Water-dispersible fullerene derivatives have a tendency toward intermolecular aggregation or spontaneous clustering in aqueous solution via π-π interaction with a size range of 25–500nm [21]. In our study, the average hydrodynamic diameter of NOTA-C60-PEG is 106nm and the size further increases to 142nm for NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD (Fig. 2 B). At the same time, ζ-potential of NOTA-C60-PEG and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD is −14.8 and 2.3mV, respectively, which also confirms the successful NOTA/RGD peptide conjugation. From the AFM images in Fig. 2A, NOTA-C60-PEG and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD form nano-scaled aggregates with the average diameter of 100–120nm, consistent with the DLS measurement and previous reports [39,40]. The small discrepancy between the sizes of C60 conjugates measured by AFM and DLS is that DLS measures the hydrodynamic diameters with extended PEG layers while the AFM measures the diameter of dried C60 conjugates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro investigation of C60 conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the interactions between C60 conjugates and cellular integrin αvβ3, the flow cytometry analysis and microscopy studies of fluorescein-C60–PEG and fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD are conducted in U87MG cells (integrin αvβ3-positive) and MCF-7 cells (integrin αvβ3-low). As seen in the FACS data (Fig. 3 A), U87MG cells incubated with 25μg/mL of fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD for 0.5h showed substantially increased cellular fluorescence compared that of fluorescein-C60-PEG at the same concentration (∼8.0-fold higher). An increase of concentration into 100μg/mL could further enhance the cellular fluorescence for both fluorescein-C60-PEG and fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD, while increase of incubation time (to 2h) did not seem to help with the cellular internalization of fluorescein-C60-PEG conjugates (Fig. S5). In comparison, neither fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD nor fluorescein-C60-PEG conjugates show obvious cellular fluorescence enhancement in MCF-7 cells at the same concentration. These results are further validated in a confocal fluorescence microscopy evaluation (Fig. 3B). Although amine-containing eight-armed PEG (positively charged) was used to modify the surface of C60, the surface positive charge of C60 conjugates was actually not very high (the ζ-potential of NOTA-C60-PEG and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD, which are −14.8 and 2.3mV, respectively). Unlike some other cationic charged nanomaterials, the toxicity of NOTA-C60-PEG and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD against U87MG cells was minimal (Fig. S6). Furthermore, fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD can be found to clearly accumulate both in cytoplasm and nuclei, but the fluorescence from nuclei is stronger, as shown in Fig. S7 as the enlarged view of a typical U87MG cell cluster. Thus, it seems that these C60 conjugates possess a nuclear transportation capacity as demonstrated by good overlay from the green fluorescence (from fluorescein) and the blue fluorescence (from 4, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) which is used for nuclei staining).

The nuclear pore complexes (NPCs) in the nuclear membrane play an important role in material exchanges between the nucleoplasm and cytoplasm. Thus, the particle size need to be sufficiently small to ensure that the nanoparticles can cross NPCs [41]. For cell nucleus targeting and penetration of nanoparticles, various kinds of nucleus localization signal (NLS) (i.e., SV40T antigen, HIV-1 TAT peptide and adenovirus) have been studied [42–44]. In this work, no NLS peptide is added on the surface of C60 conjugates. Therefore the intra-nuclei transport observed here might be primarily attributed by the ultrasmall size of disaggregated C60 unimers from the large-sized aggregates after their cellular internalization. In several previous studies, fullerene derivatives have been studied on their cellular uptake and distributions [45–47]. It is found that the hydrophobic surface of the C60 molecules can help to enhance cell uptake and binds strongly to oligodeoxynucleotide, showing efficient gene delivery ability [45]. Another study on the cell uptake of organofullerene derivatives carrying gene indicates that cell uptake occurs in the form of endosomes by microscopic observation [46]. According to a recent study, Liu, et al. reported that their fullerene-DOX conjugate was distributed mostly in the cytoplasm [47]. To the best of our knowledge, our study provides the initial evidence on the predominant nucleoplasm distribution of fullerene-based conjugates. More investigations are needed for further understanding on how to tune the structure of C60 conjugates for regulating intracellular and intranuclear transportation behaviors. By taking together these results, we can conclude that cRGD conjugation and cellular integrin αvβ3 targeting is one of the most significant contributors for cellular internalization of fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD into U87MG cells. Therefore, it provides the justification for in vivo investigation of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD.

The decay-corrected radiochemical yield is 75±7% (n=3) for the labeling of 64Cu onto NOTA-C60-PEG and NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD, following the reaction ratio of 15μg NOTA-C60-PEG or NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD per 37MBq of 64Cu. The radiochemical purity for both NOTA-C60-PEG derivatives was >95%. The specific activity of both 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG and 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD is ∼1.85GBq/mg. Since PET imaging detects the radioactive nuclides (64Cu here) instead of NOTA-C60-PEG conjugates, excellent in vivo stability is extremely important to ensure that radioactivity density acquired in PET truly matches the distribution profile from the C60 conjugates. Thus, serum stability evaluation for 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG and 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD is required. Our results indicated that more than 91% of 64Cu retained on the C60 conjugates over a 24h incubation with full mouse serum (Fig. 4 A), indicating the stability of these 64Cu-labeled C60 conjugates is sufficient for in vivo applications.

Based on our previous experience with radioactive nanomaterials [48–51], the time points of 0.5, 2, 16, and 24h p.i. are chosen to explore the in vivo distribution profiles of these 64Cu-labeled C60 conjugates in PET. The coronal PET images that contain the center of U87MG tumors are shown in Fig. 4B. Potent uptake of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD started as early as 0.5h p.i. and plateaued at 2h p.i. At the same time, significant amounts of radioactivity can also be found in the kidneys, bladder and liver for both 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD and 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG, while the excretion of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG from mice seems to be faster than that of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD. Quantitative data obtained from ROI analysis of the PET results are shown in Fig. 4C, D and Table 1 .

The accumulation of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD in the U87MG tumors is clearly visible (1.4±0.2%ID/g) at 0.5h p.i. and remains relatively stable over the time (1.4±0.3, 1.5±1.5, 1.6±0.2%ID/g at 2, 16 and 24h p.i. respectively, n=3, Fig. 4 and Table S1), providing sufficiently good contrast for tumor. The liver uptake of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD is 4.8±0.4, 5.0±2.0, 4.2±2.2 and 5.5±0.9%ID/g at 0.5, 2, 16 and 24h p.i. respectively, while the radioactivity in the blood is 4.3±1.5, 1.1±0.8, 0.8±0.3 and 0.8±0.1%ID/g at 0.5, 2, 16 and 24h p.i. respectively (n=4, Table S1). From these data, we can find that the clearance of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD from blood is much faster than that from U87MG tumors. The accumulation of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD in normal tissues (e.g., muscle) is very low. The excretion of 64Cu-labeled C60 conjugates from the test subjects is through both the hepatobiliary and renal pathway, which is consistent with a recent report on in vivo metabolic evaluation of fullerene derivatives by PET/CT [52]. The results acquired in that study indicated that radiolabeled C60 samples had longer blood retention than the Gd@C82 samples. In our study, we combine C60 molecule with highly hydrophilic PEG, a highly efficient chelating agent (NOTA) and an active targeting ligand (cRGD) to achieve tumor targeting and imaging. 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD exhibit not only good tumor contrast and but also fast renal clearance profiles (Fig. 4 and Table S1).

64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG administration exhibits significantly reduced tumor uptake during the monitored time frame (p < 0.05, n=3, Fig. 4D, E, and Table S1), which confirms that the conjugation of cRGD could be the leading reason behind the elevated accumulation of these C60 conjugates in U87MG tumors. Significantly lower liver and bladder accumulation of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG is observed compared with those from 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD (Table S1). Also, a similar blood clearance profile is demonstrated when it is compared with 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD. Fig. 4E gives an overview of the U87MG tumor uptake of these 64Cu-labeled C60 conjugates over the time. The differences between the tumor uptake of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD and the control group (i.e., 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG) were statistically significant except at 16h p.i. (P<0.05, n=3). In addition, the tumor-to-muscle (T/M) ratios increases as well. As shown in Table S2, T/M value for 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD is 4.6±1.7 at 0.5h p.i., and peaked at 24h p.i. (7.6±2.0), significantly higher than that of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG (3.1±0.2 at 24h p.i.). These T/M ratios confirm that cRGD conjugation onto C60 could result in very good tumor detection contrast.

Considering the dominant bladder signal at 0.5 and 2h p.i. directly observed from PET images, our C60 nanoconjugates exhibit the unique renal-clearance property which is rarely observed for other carbonaceous nanomaterials (i.e., nanographenes, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanodots). One hypothesis is that fullerene has a convergent spherical structure with around 1nm-diameter size. Although water-soluble C60 derivatives usually exist as the form of incompact aggregates in the aqueous environment, the small unimers might be gradually stripped from the aggregates due to the dilution in the blood stream or dispersion effects of the dynamic laminar blood flow and could gradually pass through the kidney and accumulate in the bladder which is finally observed by the PET imaging. This phenomenon is similar to some renal clearable inorganic nanoparticles (e.g., GS-[198Au]AuNPs with hydrodynamic diameter of 3.0nm), which are also found to excrete primarily into the bladder [53].

The blood concentration of 64Cu-labeled C60 conjugates in vivo is converted by PET ROIs dynamic analysis. As shown in Fig. 5 , the blood concentration decreases sharply within the first 30min to reach a plateau. The data of blood concentration versus time for each mouse is analyzed by DAS 2.0. All results are expressed as mean±SD. Based on the results from Table 1 and 2 it can be seen that the Pharmacokinetic data are better fitted by the three compartment open model with weight of 1/cc (Fig. 5). The calculated pharmacokinetic parameters of three compartment model are listed in Table 3 .

As a common pharmacokinetics viewpoint, all of models are raw simplifications of the physiological reality. In the typical three compartment model, the central (1st) compartment usually represents blood, whereas compartment 2 can be attributed to an extracellular space of the body (ECF), and compartment 3 to the deep tissue compartment where the radiolabeled C60-cRGD conjugate is strongly bound to integrin αvβ3 receptor in this case [54–55]. Fast distribution phase α is assumed to represent radiopharmaceutical passing from the blood (central compartment) to the ECF (2nd compartment). The slow distribution phase β is assumed to describe radiopharmaceutical passing from the ECF (2nd compartment) to the tumor (3rd compartment). The elimination phase γ indicates the clearance of the radiopharmaceutical from the body. In this study, the distribution-phase half-life (t 1/2
α and t 1/2
β ) of intravenously administered 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD is about 1.007min and 7.596min (Table 3), respectively. This indicates that C60 conjugates reach the periphery compartment rapidly in pace with the blood circulation of mice. While the elimination-phase half-life (t 1/2
γ ) is relatively long (69.315min), which allows C60 conjugates to circulate in the blood at a relatively high concentration and continuously extravasate from the blood vessels into the tumor, increasing the cRGD-mediated ligand-receptor binding probability. The pharmacokinetic profile of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD is quite similar to a reported renal clearable cysteine-QDs with a blood elimination half-life of 48min [15].

Fig. 6 A shows the organ distribution data from γ-counting at 24h p.i. The organ distribution profiles match very well with PET ROI quantification, confirming that PET imaging can accurately reflect the distribution pattern of these 64Cu-labeled C60 conjugates. The statistically different accumulation between 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG and 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD in liver, kidneys, and spleen reveals faster clearance of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG in most organs and U87MG tumors still have significantly higher accumulation of 64Cu-NOTA-C60-PEG-cRGD.

Histological examination is adopted to give further evidence for the distribution of C60 conjugates in different tissues/organs. U87MG tumor-bearing mice injected with fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD and fluorescein-C60-PEG are euthanized at 16h p.i., and then U87MG tumors, liver, spleen, and muscle are frozen and cryo-sectioned for the immunofluorescence staining. For the immunohistology analysis, the strong fluorescence from fluorescein (on the structure of fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD and fluorescein-C60-PEG) serves localizer for these C60 conjugates. As indicated in Fig.6B, there are substantial amounts of fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD conjugates accumulating in U87MG tumor (green fluorescence is significantly higher than that from fluorescein-C60-PEG), liver and spleen, which is consistent with the PET imaging results. Moreover, the green fluorescence of fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD in the liver and spleen exhibits very weak overlaps with integrin αvβ3 (red fluorescence), suggesting that the uptake of fluorescein-C60-PEG-cRGD in these organs is very likely coming from non-specific capture by MPS (e.g., macrophages).

In conclusion, we have reported the synthesis and characterization of C60 conjugates for a potential cancer diagnostic application. The pharmacokinetics, tumor-targeting efficacy and specificity of the fullerene-based conjugates are assessed by serial quantitative PET imaging after intravenous administration in U87MG tumor-bearing mice. The in vitro cellular uptake, in vivo PET imaging, ex vivo biodistribution and immunohistology studies agree well with each other, all indicating that cRGD-conjugated C60 nanoparticles exhibit stronger tumor-targeting ability than cRGD-free C60 nanoparticles, due to integrin αvβ3-mediated ligand-receptor active targeting. The pharmacokinetic profiles of fullerene-based nanoparticles conjugated with cRGD and NOTA are better fitted to the three compartment model, which is similar to several reported renal clearable inorganic nanoparticles. These results suggest that the C60-based conjugates can serve as a safe nanocarrier for future clinical applications with fast clearance behaviors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support of this work by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 81402877 (T.C.)], the Program for Jiangsu Province Innovative Research Team and the National Institutes of Health NIH/NCI P01 CA085878 (H.H.).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.actbio.2017.08.011.
